workout.title: Chest Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
instructions: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
Lie supine on the incline bench with your head supported, feet flat on the floor, and back slightly arched while maintaining scapular retraction.
Grip the barbell with a pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your upper chest/shoulder line, then stabilize before starting the descent.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the upper chest/clavicular area, keeping elbows at roughly a 30–60° angle from the torso (avoid flaring to 90°).
Lightly touch or stop just above the chest, pause briefly without bouncing, then exhale and press the bar upward and slightly back to the start position, fully extending the elbows.
Keep core braced and feet planted throughout to maintain stability and prevent excessive lower back extension.
Carefully re-rack the bar under control or have a spotter assist when finished.
exercises.description: A barbell press performed on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest by pressing the bar upward and slightly toward the clavicle. It also engages the front delts and triceps for pressing and locking out the weight.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back.
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at your thighs.

Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back. Retract your shoulder blades and keep them contacting the bench.
Start with the dumbbells at mid-chest, elbows bent about 90 degrees and wrists neutral (dumbbell handles aligned with forearms).
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly straight but not hyperextended; keep wrists stacked over elbows.

Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position, maintaining control and stopping when the dumbbells reach chest level or your elbows form ~90 degrees. Keep elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from your torso to protect the shoulders.
Keep your core braced and feet firmly planted for stability throughout each rep; avoid excessive flaring of the ribs or letting the shoulders lift off the bench.
Use a spotter for heavy loads and choose a weight that allows controlled full-range reps with good form.
exercises.description: Flat dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a bench, pressing two dumbbells upward from chest level to develop the pectorals; the movement also recruits the anterior delts and triceps for stability and lockout.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles or place your forearms against the pads with a slight bend in the elbows; start with arms out to the sides and shoulder blades pulled back and down.
Exhale and drive the handles forward and together in a smooth arc by contracting the pectorals, keeping the elbows slightly bent and wrists neutral.
Pause briefly when the hands/handles meet or are directly in front of your chest, feeling the squeeze in the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly allow the levers to return to the start position under control, maintaining shoulder blade stability and avoiding rapid letting go or trunk leaning.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; use a weight that allows full control of both concentric and eccentric phases without excessive shoulder or back movement.
exercises.description: Seated machine chest fly performed on a lever/pec-deck: you bring the arms together in front of the chest against resistance to isolate the pectoral muscles. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and controlled arc to emphasize the inner chest.

exercises.name: Cable Middle Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to about mid-chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to about mid-chest height and select an appropriate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered stance (one foot slightly forward) with a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip and extend your arms out to the sides so there is tension on the cables; keep a small, constant bend in the elbows.
Retract your shoulder blades and keep the shoulders down; brace your core to prevent torso movement.
Exhale and bring the handles together in front of your chest in a wide arc, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Inhale and slowly reverse the motion with control, allowing the arms to return to the starting position until you feel a gentle stretch in the chest.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding torso leaning, shoulder shrugging, or locking the elbows.
exercises.description: Standing bilateral cable fly performed with the pulleys set at mid-chest height; the exercise horizontally adducts the arms in a controlled arc to load and contract the pectorals. A staggered stance and slight elbow bend are used to stabilize and protect the joints while the chest performs the work.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.notes: Light weight, focus on form and range of motion

instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and extend the arms straight above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement (do not lock the elbows).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, maintaining the slight elbow bend until you feel a stretch in the chest or your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep the shoulder blades stable on the bench (no excessive shrugging) and avoid letting the dumbbells travel behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then exhale and bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc, focusing on squeezing the pectorals until the weights meet above the sternum.
Perform each rep with controlled tempo and avoid bouncing or using momentum; select a weight that allows full control and a safe range of motion.
exercises.description: A supine resistance exercise performed with dumbbells that isolates the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from above the sternum to the sides, producing a deep stretch and concentric squeeze of the pectorals.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench between two cable towers (about 30–45° incline).
instructions: Set an incline bench between two cable towers (about 30–45° incline). Attach single D‑handles and set the pulleys to low or just below chest height so the cables travel upward across your chest.
Sit on the bench and lie back so your head rests on the pad; grip one handle in each hand with a neutral or palms‑facing posture and feet flat on the floor.
Retract your shoulder blades and brace your core so your back stays pressed into the bench (inclined supine position). Keep a slight, fixed bend in your elbows throughout the exercise.
Start with your arms out to the sides in line with your chest (open position) and inhale, then exhale as you bring the handles together in a wide arc toward the centerline of your upper chest.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction, focusing on squeezing the pectorals and keeping the elbows slightly bent and stable (avoid driving motion from the wrists or excessive shoulder elevation).
Slowly reverse the movement on the inhale, controlling the cables as you return to the start position until you feel a stretch in the chest without losing scapular retraction.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining core engagement and avoiding excessive momentum or arching of the lower back.
exercises.description: Lying on an incline bench placed between two cable towers, you perform a bilateral fly motion with a slight elbow bend to concentrically bring the handles together and target the upper chest. The movement emphasizes a horizontal adduction arc while the core stabilizes the torso.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered or shoulder-width stance with knees soft and core braced (slight forward lean at the hips).
Reach down and grab each handle with a neutral or slightly supinated grip, palms facing in or up, elbows slightly bent and fixed in that position throughout the movement.
Begin with the arms out to the sides and low (handles near hips or knees) and inhale.
Exhale and pull the handles upward and inward in a wide arc, keeping the elbows slightly bent, bringing the hands together or in front of the chest/upper sternum.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze the pectorals while avoiding excessive shoulder shrugging.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, allowing the cables to return the arms to the start position while maintaining tension in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions and re-rack safely; keep torso stable and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly is a low-to-high cable chest fly that targets the pectorals by sweeping the arms upward and together from low pulley positions; performed standing with a slight forward lean and a soft bend in the elbows to emphasize the chest contraction.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Alternate Chest Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to approximately chest height and attach single-hand handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to approximately chest height and attach single-hand handles.
Stand in the center of the cable machine in a staggered (split) stance with one foot forward and a slight bend in both knees; lean slightly forward from the hips to engage the core.
Grasp both handles with a neutral grip and step forward until there is tension in the cables; hold both arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows and shoulders down and back.
Keep the torso stable and core braced; allow one handle to remain out while you perform the fly with the other arm.

Exhale and bring the working hand in a horizontal arc across your body toward the midline, finishing with the palm facing your opposite shoulder or hands meeting in front of the sternum; maintain that slight elbow bend throughout the movement.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction, squeezing the pectorals, then slowly reverse the motion under control until the arm returns to the start position.
Alternate to the other arm after completing the prescribed reps for the first arm, keeping the non-working arm either extended or lightly resisting to maintain symmetry and tension.
Maintain steady controlled tempo, avoid torso rotation or using momentum, and keep shoulders down (not shrugged) throughout the set.
exercises.description: A standing cable fly performed one arm at a time to isolate the chest by bringing a single handle across the body in a controlled arc while the other arm returns. Use a staggered stance and slight forward lean to maintain balance and core stability.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Fly
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
instructions: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered (split) stance or feet shoulder-width apart with a slight forward lean from the hips so you have stable balance; brace your core.
Grasp a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip and take one step forward until you feel light tension in the cables; keep a small bend in the elbows throughout.
Start with your arms out to the sides roughly at shoulder height (or slightly higher for a high-cable fly) and chest lifted; retract your shoulder blades slightly to protect the joints.
Exhale and bring the handles together in a controlled arc in front of your chest at midline, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the finish.
Pause briefly with hands meeting or slightly overlapping, then inhale and slowly resist the cables as you return your arms to the start position using the same arcing path.
Keep the movement smooth and avoid locking the elbows, excessive torso rotation, or shrugging the shoulders; maintain core tension for stability.
exercises.description: Standing cable fly performed with the pulleys set high to mid-high engages the chest by bringing the arms together in a wide arc; the shoulders and core stabilize the movement. It emphasizes the pectoral fibers with controlled concentric adduction and slow eccentric return.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Cable Wide Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the wide bar with a pronated (overhand) grip wider than shoulder width.
Sit tall with a slight natural arch in the lower back, feet flat, chest up and head neutral.
Begin the rep by inhaling, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back toward your ribcage.
Lead the motion with your elbows and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom, bringing the bar to the upper chest or collarbone level.
Pause briefly while maintaining tension in the lats, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive backward lean or swinging.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with controlled extension of the arms until you feel a full stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and controlled breathing throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip lat pulldown using an overhand wide bar to pull the cable down toward the upper chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus dorsi. Performed seated with knees braced to isolate the back and minimize leg drive.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
Grasp one or two handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) or overhand grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; keep a neutral neck and spine.
Slightly bend the knees and brace your core to stabilize the torso; do not lean forward or backward.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears, focusing on contracting the trapezius—do not roll or rotate the shoulders.
Hold the peak contraction briefly (about 1 second) while maintaining straight arms and a neutral neck.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start in a controlled manner until traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movement strict and avoiding momentum; reduce weight if you must use arm or torso movement to lift.
exercises.description: Standing cable shrugs target the trapezius by elevating the shoulders against resistance from a cable. Performed with arms straight at the sides, the movement emphasizes a vertical shrug with controlled lowering.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Lever Front Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine weight and set the seat height so your feet are flat on the floor and the thigh pads rest snugly on your thighs to prevent lifting.
instructions: Adjust the machine weight and set the seat height so your feet are flat on the floor and the thigh pads rest snugly on your thighs to prevent lifting.
Sit upright on the seat (seated), reach up and grasp the front/wide handles with an overhand (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder width; arms should start almost fully extended.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back (scapular depression/retraction) before initiating the pull to engage the lats.
Exhale and pull the handles straight down toward the upper chest/front, leading with your elbows and keeping your torso mostly upright (allow a very slight lean back but avoid excessive swinging).
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the bottom of the movement, keeping the wrists neutral and elbows pointing down/back, then pause briefly.
Inhale and slowly control the weight as you extend your arms back up to the start position, allowing the shoulder blades to fully stretch without shrugging the shoulders.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping motion smooth and avoiding momentum or pulling with the neck; lower weight if you cannot maintain full scapular control.
exercises.description: Seated lever front pulldown using a wide overhead handle to pull resistance down in front of the chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus region. Performed seated with thigh pads to stabilize the body while pulling the handles to the upper chest.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Cable Low Seated Row
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulley to the low position and select an appropriate weight; sit on the bench or seat with feet braced on the foot platform and knees slightly bent.
instructions: Set the cable pulley to the low position and select an appropriate weight; sit on the bench or seat with feet braced on the foot platform and knees slightly bent.
Grasp the handle with both hands using a neutral or overhand grip, then sit upright with a neutral spine and chest lifted; brace your abs.
Start with your arms extended and shoulders relaxed forward (the starting position).
Exhale and pull the handle toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together — keep elbows close to your body.
Avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum; maintain a stable, upright torso (a small, controlled lean is acceptable but do not rock).
Pause briefly at the peak contraction while maintaining scapular retraction and a tight core.
Inhale and slowly extend your arms to return the handle to the start position with control, allowing the shoulders to protract but not round excessively.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping movements smooth and controlled; lower the weight if you find yourself shrugging or swinging.
exercises.description: Seated low cable row is a horizontal-pulling exercise performed from a seated position that primarily develops the lats while also engaging the mid- and upper-back and arm muscles. It emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive to pull the handle toward the torso.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Arm Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.
instructions: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.

Stand facing the machine about 1–2 feet away, feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent; hinge forward at the hips so your torso is angled forward (standing bent-over position) with a neutral spine.
Reach up and grasp the bar with an overhand grip (palms facing down) about shoulder-width apart; maintain a small, natural bend in the elbows throughout (do not lock).
Brace your core and depress your shoulder blades slightly (avoid shrugging) so tension is felt in the lats before starting the pull.

Exhale and pull the bar down in a controlled arc by extending the shoulders and pulling with the lats until the bar reaches your upper thighs or mid-thigh level; keep the arms long and the elbows fixed in their slight bend.
Squeeze the lats and hold the bottom position briefly while keeping the ribs down and spine neutral.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to rise back to the start position under control, resisting the cable so the lats remain under tension; do not let the lower back round as you return.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining the hip hinge, neutral spine, and long-arm position on every repetition.
exercises.description: Standing cable straight-arm pulldowns hinge from the hips and pull a high cable down with mostly straight arms to load the lats through a long-range extension. The movement emphasizes scapular depression and shoulder extension while keeping the torso braced.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright (feet about hip-width), hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip at your sides, knees softly bent and core braced.
instructions: Stand upright (feet about hip-width), hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip at your sides, knees softly bent and core braced.
Keep your arms straight (no shrugging with the elbows) and maintain a neutral spine and head position throughout.
Exhale and lift (shrug) both shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as you can, focusing on contracting the top of the traps.
Pause and squeeze at the top for 1–2 seconds, avoiding rolling or rotating the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the starting position with control until arms are fully relaxed at your sides.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping movement vertical and controlled; increase weight only if you can maintain form.
exercises.description: Standing exercise using dumbbells to raise the shoulders toward the ears, primarily working the trapezius muscles to develop upper-back and neck strength. Performed with a short vertical shrugging motion while keeping the arms straight.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

warmup.notes: Brief warmup to activate target muscles. Can skip if first working set feels warm.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Back Seated Row
exercises.notes: Set your spine in a neutral (straight) position with chest up and shoulders relaxed—avoid rounding the back or excessive leaning.
instructions: Sit on the bench with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the close/V-handle with both hands and sit tall.
Set your spine in a neutral (straight) position with chest up and shoulders relaxed—avoid rounding the back or excessive leaning.
Begin the repetition by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction) to initiate the movement.
Continue pulling the handle toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows straight back; keep elbows close to the body.
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the end range for 1 second while maintaining an upright seated position (do not hyperextend the lower back).
Return the handle slowly under control by extending the arms and allowing the scapulae to protract until arms are nearly straight.
Breathe out as you pull, inhale as you return; use a controlled tempo and avoid using torso momentum to complete the reps.
exercises.description: Seated cable row performed with a straight, neutral spine; pull the handle toward the torso using shoulder blade retraction to emphasize the lats and upper back. Maintain an upright torso and controlled tempo throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Lever Supinated Grip Seated Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Load the desired plates and set the chest pad/seat so your chest rests against the pad with arms fully extended when you reach the handles; sit upright with feet braced on the platform or footrests.
instructions: Load the desired plates and set the chest pad/seat so your chest rests against the pad with arms fully extended when you reach the handles; sit upright with feet braced on the platform or footrests.
Grasp the handles with a supinated (palms-up/underhand) grip, wrists neutral and shoulder blades relaxed forward at the start.
Brace your core, keep your chest on the pad and maintain a neutral spine (no excessive arching or leaning forward).
Exhale and pull the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows straight back—lead with the elbows rather than the hands.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together and feel the contraction in your lats and upper back at the end range; keep the wrists and forearms steady.
Pause for a brief second at peak contraction while maintaining an upright, chest-supported position.
Inhale and slowly extend your arms back to the starting position with controlled movement until your elbows are almost straight, keeping tension on the muscles.
Repeat for the prescribed reps; avoid using torso momentum or excessive shoulder elevation—focus on controlled scapular retraction and full range.
exercises.description: Seated row performed on a plate-loaded lever machine using a supinated (underhand) grip; the movement pulls the handles toward the torso to drive horizontal shoulder extension and scapular retraction. It emphasizes the lats while also loading the arms and upper back musculature.

exercises.name: Assisted Close grip Underhand Chin-up
exercises.notes: Adjust the assisted machine weight so you have the desired level of assistance and step onto the knee pad; kneel with both knees on the pad and feet behind you.
instructions: Adjust the assisted machine weight so you have the desired level of assistance and step onto the knee pad; kneel with both knees on the pad and feet behind you.
Reach up and grasp the bar with an underhand (palms facing you) close grip, hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower; maintain a neutral head and upright torso.
Start from a dead hang with arms fully extended and shoulders relaxed, bracing your core to prevent excessive swinging.
Drive your elbows down and back, retracting your shoulder blades and pulling your chest toward the bar until your chin clears the bar or your chest meets the bar.
Pause briefly at the top while squeezing the lats and biceps, then lower yourself slowly and controlled back to full arm extension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping motion smooth and avoiding kipping or swinging; adjust assistance as needed between sets.
exercises.description: A close-grip underhand (supinated) chin-up performed on an assisted pull-up machine that emphasizes the lats while increasing biceps involvement; the machine pad provides counterweight assistance to reduce the effective load. The movement pulls the torso up until the chin clears the bar and then lowers under control to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Lever High Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the machine with feet flat on the floor and chest/torso stabilized against the pad (or chest pad if present).
instructions: Sit upright on the machine with feet flat on the floor and chest/torso stabilized against the pad (or chest pad if present). Adjust seat so handles are at or slightly above shoulder height when arms are extended.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, arms extended and shoulders relaxed. Take a full breath and brace your core.

Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the handles down and back toward the upper chest/near clavicle, leading with the elbows and keeping them at roughly shoulder height (elbows flared).
Squeeze the shoulder blades and upper back at the end of the concentric phase for 1 second, keeping the torso still and avoiding excessive trunk lean or momentum.
Slowly extend the arms back to the start position under control (eccentric phase), maintaining tension in the upper back and preventing the weights from slamming.
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out during the pull and inhale during the release.

Common form tips: keep the neck neutral, avoid shrugging the shoulders up, don’t use excessive body swing, and adjust seat/handle height so the pull targets the upper back rather than purely the lats.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded high row where you pull overhead/forward handles toward the upper chest to emphasize the upper back (rhomboids, mid traps and rear delts) while also engaging lats and arm muscles. The torso is stabilized against a pad and movement is performed in a controlled pull-and-release.

exercises.name: Cable Bar Lateral Pulldown
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the lat pulldown bench with feet flat on the floor and knees secured under the thigh pads.
instructions: Sit upright on the lat pulldown bench with feet flat on the floor and knees secured under the thigh pads.
Reach up and take a wide, pronated (overhand) grip on the cable bar, hands wider than shoulder width.
Sit tall with chest up and a slight natural arch in the lower back; lean back slightly at the hips (about 10–20 degrees) to create a stable pulling angle.
Exhale and pull the bar straight down toward the upper chest, driving the elbows down and back and leading the movement with the elbows rather than the hands.
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the bottom of the movement, keeping the shoulders down and avoiding shrugging.
Pause briefly (about 1 second) at the bottom while maintaining tension, then inhale and slowly return the bar to the fully extended starting position with controlled resistance.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while avoiding excessive torso swing or momentum and keeping the wrists neutral.
exercises.description: Seated cable bar lateral pulldown using a wide pronated grip to pull the bar down toward the upper chest, emphasizing vertical adduction of the arms to load the latissimus dorsi and upper back. Performed seated with knees braced under the pads and a controlled, full range of motion.

exercises.name: Barbell Back Wide Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent.
instructions: Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent.
Place a loaded barbell across the back of your hips/upper thighs or behind your body and take a wide, overhand grip (hands wider than shoulder-width).
Keep your chest up, core braced, neck neutral and arms straight (no elbow bend) throughout the movement.
Raise (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible, focusing on contracting the traps—do not roll the shoulders.
Pause briefly at the top while squeezing the traps.
Slowly lower the shoulders back down to the starting position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a stable torso and avoiding forward/backward lean.
exercises.description: Standing barbell shrug performed with a wide, behind-the-back grip to place emphasis on the upper traps; elevate the shoulders straight up toward the ears while keeping the arms extended and torso stable.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Arnold Press
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent and palms facing your torso (palms-in, front-rack position).
Initiate the movement by rotating your wrists outward as you begin to press the dumbbells upward, so palms face forward at the top of the movement.
Press the weights overhead until your arms are nearly straight (avoid locking the elbows), keeping control and a straight line from wrists to elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with palms facing forward, shoulders engaged but not shrugged.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back down while rotating the wrists inward so palms return to facing your torso and the dumbbells return to the starting position at shoulder height.
Maintain an upright torso and braced core throughout; avoid excessive arching of the lower back or using leg drive to lift the weights.
Breathe out while pressing up and inhale while lowering. Perform controlled repetitions with full rotation on each rep.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell Arnold press is a rotating overhead press that targets the delts while involving the triceps and upper back for support. It begins with palms facing the body at shoulder level and rotates the wrists as you press overhead to emphasize the front and side deltoid heads.

exercises.name: Barbell Seated Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright near the middle of a flat bench (no back support) with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and knees bent.
instructions: Sit upright near the middle of a flat bench (no back support) with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and knees bent.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width so your wrists are stacked over your elbows; rest the bar on your upper chest/clavicle area.
Brace your core and pull your shoulder blades slightly down and back to create a stable upper-back platform; keep a neutral spine and a small natural lumbar curve (avoid excessive arching).
Unrack the bar (or lift it off the rack) and hold it just above the clavicle with elbows slightly forward of the bar and forearms vertical.

Exhale and press the bar straight up in a controlled line over the midline of your head until your arms are fully extended but not aggressively hyperextended; the bar should finish roughly over your shoulders/ears.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged, then inhale and lower the bar under control back to the starting position at the upper chest/clavicle.
Repeat for desired reps while maintaining a tight core, flat feet, and minimal torso lean; stop or rack the bar safely if form breaks or you cannot control the descent.
exercises.description: Seated barbell overhead press performed from a bench to drive the bar straight overhead, primarily working the shoulder delts while also engaging the triceps and upper back for stabilization. The movement emphasizes a vertical press with a braced core to limit excessive spine extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Dumbbell One Arm Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft; hold a dumbbell in one hand at shoulder height with elbow bent and the palm facing forward or neutral.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft; hold a dumbbell in one hand at shoulder height with elbow bent and the palm facing forward or neutral. Place the free hand on your hip to help limit torso rotation.
Brace your core and gently squeeze your glutes to prevent low-back arching; keep the chest up and head neutral.
Exhale and press the dumbbell straight overhead by extending the arm, keeping the elbow under the wrist and the motion controlled.
Pause briefly at the top with the arm nearly straight (do not hyperextend the elbow) and keep the shoulder depressed (avoid shrugging up toward the ear).
Inhale and lower the dumbbell slowly and under control back to the starting position at shoulder height.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, then switch sides. Reduce weight if you notice excessive torso lean, back arching, or loss of control.
exercises.description: A single-arm overhead dumbbell press performed standing to develop shoulder (delts) strength and stability while engaging the triceps and core for control. The opposite hand is often placed on the hip to limit torso rotation.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Row
exercises.notes: Set up in a staggered, bench-supported position: place your left knee and left hand on a flat bench (all-fours/bench-assisted), right foot on the floor for stability; hold a dumbbell in your right hand with the arm hanging straight down.
instructions: Set up in a staggered, bench-supported position: place your left knee and left hand on a flat bench (all-fours/bench-assisted), right foot on the floor for stability; hold a dumbbell in your right hand with the arm hanging straight down.
Hinge at the hips so your torso is nearly parallel to the floor, keep a neutral spine and your gaze a few inches ahead of the bench.

With a slight bend in the elbow, externally rotate the shoulder and drive the elbow out to the side (away from the body) so the dumbbell is pulled up toward shoulder/upper-chest level; lead the movement with the elbow and focus on squeezing the rear delt.
Pause for 1 second at the top while maintaining scapular control (do not shrug), then slowly lower the dumbbell back to the start under control until the arm is nearly extended.
Breathe out as you lift and inhale as you lower; perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat with the opposite arm using the same bench-supported setup.
Coaching cues: avoid rotating the torso, keep the wrist neutral, use a light-to-moderate weight to maintain strict form, and emphasize a controlled eccentric (lowering) phase.
exercises.description: A single-arm, bench-supported row performed with the elbow flared to isolate the rear delts; it emphasizes posterior shoulder activation while also engaging upper-back muscles. Performed with a neutral spine and controlled tempo to maximize rear delt contraction.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Stand feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
instructions: Stand feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor, keep a neutral spine and knees slightly bent (bent-over position).
Let your arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a slight bend in the elbows; maintain a braced core and avoid rounding the back.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the ground (level with the shoulders).
Squeeze the rear delts and shoulder blades together at the top for a brief pause, avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start under control, resisting any momentum; repeat for desired reps.
Keep the movement strict: use lighter weight if you must swing the torso or bend the elbows excessively, and breathe out on the lift and in on the lower.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell raise that isolates the rear (posterior) deltoids by lifting the arms out to the sides while the torso is hinged forward. It emphasizes shoulder horizontal abduction and scapular retraction with a controlled tempo.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: Barbell Seated Military Press (inside squat cage)
exercises.notes: Set a flat bench inside the squat cage and adjust the safety pins/holders so the bar can be unracked and racked at shoulder height; sit with your back firmly against the bench and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Set a flat bench inside the squat cage and adjust the safety pins/holders so the bar can be unracked and racked at shoulder height; sit with your back firmly against the bench and feet flat on the floor.
Grip the bar with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder width, wrists stacked over elbows and chest tall with a neutral spine; brace your abs.
Unrack the bar and hold it at upper-chest/chin level with elbows slightly forward of the bar path and eyes forward; take a full breath and brace.
Press the bar straight upward in a controlled path, exhaling as you drive until your arms are fully extended overhead without locking aggressively or overarching the lower back.
Lower the bar under control back to the starting position at about upper-chest/chin level while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking and the torso stable.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully return the bar to the rack pins and ensure it is secure before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: Seated barbell military press performed inside a squat cage that focuses on vertical pressing to develop the delts while seated to limit leg drive. The movement involves pressing the bar overhead from about upper-chest/chin level and lowering it under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and wrists straight.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the movement and maintain a neutral spine and chest up.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by lifting at the shoulders, leading with the elbows so the hands finish roughly at shoulder height (arms nearly parallel to the floor).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears; keep the neck long and scapulae stable to minimize excessive trap involvement.
Pause briefly (0–1 second) at the top while maintaining tension in the delts.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movement smooth and avoiding momentum or swinging; use lighter weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell lateral raise works the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides to about shoulder height, with a slight elbow bend and controlled motion. It improves shoulder width and isolates the middle shoulder fibers while the traps and forearms assist.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Reverse Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and position it so you can lie face down with your chest supported and feet on the floor or toes braced behind you (prone on bench).
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and position it so you can lie face down with your chest supported and feet on the floor or toes braced behind you (prone on bench).
Pick up a pair of light-to-moderate dumbbells and hold them with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) with arms hanging straight down beneath your shoulders.
Tuck your chin slightly so your neck stays neutral and pull your shoulder blades down and back (scapular retraction) before starting the rep.

With a slight bend in the elbows, raise the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor; lead the movement from the shoulders (posterior delts), not the hands.
Pause and squeeze the shoulder blades and traps at the top for 1 second, keeping the torso stable on the bench and avoiding trunk rotation or swinging.
Lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting position, maintaining the slight elbow bend and scapular control.
Repeat for the desired reps, breathing out as you lift and in as you lower. Reduce weight if you must use momentum to complete the reps.
exercises.description: Chest-supported dumbbell reverse fly performed prone on an incline bench that isolates the posterior delts while also engaging the upper back and traps for scapular retraction. Use a controlled, wide-arc motion with a slight elbow bend to avoid momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise (support head)
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench so the pad is angled and stand facing the bench; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and rest your forehead (or chest/upper stomach) lightly against the bench pad to support the head and torso.
instructions: Set an incline bench so the pad is angled and stand facing the bench; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and rest your forehead (or chest/upper stomach) lightly against the bench pad to support the head and torso.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and let the arms hang straight down beneath your shoulders; maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Begin the movement by initiating at the shoulders: raise both dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc with a slight bend in the elbows, leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Continue lifting until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (or in line with your shoulders), keeping the wrists neutral and avoiding excessive elbow lock.
Pause and squeeze the rear delts/upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the head and torso supported on the bench.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position under control, resisting any tendency to let momentum drop the weights.
Breathe out as you lift and inhale as you lower. Maintain a stable torso—do not rotate, jerk, or shrug the shoulders during the movement.
Use lighter weight if you cannot perform the motion with controlled form or if you feel neck or lumbar strain despite the head support.
exercises.description: A bent-over rear lateral raise performed with the head supported on an incline bench to isolate the posterior delts; lift dumbbells out to the sides while keeping the torso stable to target the rear shoulder and upper back. This variation reduces momentum and neck strain by using the bench for support.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Supinated Face Pull (with rope)
exercises.notes: Set a cable pulley to about eye to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
instructions: Set a cable pulley to about eye to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
Stand facing the machine in a staggered or hip-width stance with a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine.
Reach forward and grab the rope ends with a supinated (palms-up) grip so your thumbs point toward you; step back until there is tension in the cable and your arms are nearly extended.
Brace your core, exhale, and pull the rope toward your face by driving your elbows out to the sides and externally rotating the shoulders so the hands move toward the ears.
At the end of the movement squeeze the shoulder blades together and pause 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso stable (avoid leaning back or shrugging).
Inhale and slowly extend the arms forward to the start position under control, maintaining tension in the cable.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping tempo controlled and focusing on rear delt and upper-back contraction rather than using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable face pull with a supinated (palms-up) rope grip emphasizes the rear delts while bringing the shoulder blades together and externally rotating the shoulders. Pull the rope toward your face with elbows high, then return under control.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Face Pull
exercises.notes: Set a cable pulley to about eye- to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
instructions: Set a cable pulley to about eye- to upper-chest height and attach a rope handle.
Stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart or in a slight staggered stance, knees soft and spine neutral.
Grasp the rope with both hands using a neutral/overhand grip (palms facing inward) and take a step back so there is tension on the cable.
Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the rope toward your face, driving the elbows out to the sides so your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
As you reach the end range, externally rotate the shoulders so the hands split apart and the rope ends are near your temples or ears; pause and squeeze the rear delts and upper back.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, extending the arms forward while maintaining scapular control until the hands return to the start position.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping torso stable (no excessive leaning or momentum) and focusing on quality of movement and external rotation.
exercises.description: A standing cable face pull with a rope attachment that emphasizes the posterior delts and upper back by pulling the rope toward the face while externally rotating the shoulders. It develops rear shoulder strength, scapular retraction, and shoulder external rotators.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
